# Usage and style

;wip_warn;


## "There is a ..." sentences.

In English the plain existence of an indefinite ;mubtada; is expressed using the word "there". For example:

+ "There is a gloom in the house."
+ "There is a type of jealousy which is liked and [there is] a type of jealousy which is disliked.
+ "There are reasons."
+ "There is a god."
+ "Is there food?"

The word "there" in these examples does not indicate a specific place. Rather it signifies the existence of the ;mubtada; of the sentence. 
This use of "there" is called the _existential_ "there".^[See also [@hapselmath_existential@](https://doi.org/10.58079/nswb)]

<!--As if to say:

i.   "A gloom exists in the house."
ii.  "A type of confidence exists which is good and a type of confidence exists which is bad.
iii. "Reasons exist"
iv.  "A god exists."
-->

Expressing such sentences in Arabic can sometimes be tricky.
There is a modern tendency to use the [Parf makan]{.trn2} [هُنَاكَ]{.ar} and the [majhUl]{.trn2} verb [يُوجَدُ]{.ar}. <!-- as direct equivalents of the English "there" and "exists".-->
So one might find:

(@) [هُنَاكَ حَزَنٌ فِي ٱلْبَيتِ.]{.ar} or  
    [يُوجَدُ حَزَنٌ فِي ٱلْبَيتِ.]{.ar}  
    [There is a gloom in the house.]{.trans}  

(@) [هناك غيرة يستحب وهناك غيرة يكره.]{.ar}  or  
    [توجد غيرة تستحب وتوجد غيرة تكره.]{.ar}  
    [There is a type of jealousy which is liked and there is a type of jealousy which is disliked.]{.trans}  

(@) [هُناك أسباب.]{.ar} or  
    [تُوجَدُ أسباب.]{.ar}  
    [There are reasons.]{.trans}  

(@) [هناك إله.]{.ar} or  
    [يوجد إله.]{.ar}  
    [There is a god.]{.trans}  

(@) [هَل هناك طعام؟]{.ar} or  
    [هل يوجد طعام؟]{.ar}  
    [Is there food?]{.trans}  

Sometimes in place of [هُنَاكَ]{.ar}, its synonyms [ثَمَّ]{.ar} or [ثَمَّةَ]{.ar} is used.
These usages of [هُنَاكَ]{.ar}, [ثَمَّ]{.ar}/[ثَمَّةَ]{.ar}, and [يُوجَدُ]{.ar} are foreign to Arabic and should generally be avoided.

In Classical Arabic, expressing such sentences falls under the category of sentences with indefinite ;mubtada;s.
We have discussed this topic in chapter\ \@ref(chap-indef-subjects).

There are various strategies for expressing such sentences:

### Using or manufacturing scope

If, for example, there is a [jArr wa-majrUr]{.trn2}, or other [cibh jumlah]{.trn2} then it can readily be used as a [xabar]{.trn2} that precedes the [mubtadaE]{.trn2}. For example:

(@) [فِي ٱلْبَيتِ حَزَنٌ.]{.ar}  
    [In the house is gloom.]{.trans}

Sometimes, a [jArr wa-majrUr]{.trn2}, or other [cibh jumlah]{.trn2} is not original, but can readily be manufactured. 
For example, in the sentence, "There are reasons." the reasons must be for something, and that something can be used as a [xabar]{.trn2}:

(@) [لِلْأَمْرِ أَسْبَابٌ.]{.ar}  
    [For the situation, are reasons.]{.trans}

### Using or manufacturing an introductory sentence

Similarly, a introductory sentence or [cibh jumlah]{.trn2} can be manufactured to pave the way for the main sentence. For example:

<!--
[الغَضَبُ غَضَبَانِ: غَضَبٌ مُسْتَحَبٌّ وَغَضَبٌ مَكْرُوهٌ.]{.ar}  
"Anger is (actually) two angers: an anger that is liked, and an anger that is disliked."

[مَنَ ٱلْغَضَبِ مَا يٌسْتَحَبُّ وَمَا يٌكْرَهُ.]{.ar}  
"From anger is that which is liked, that which is disliked."
-->

(@ex000140) [مِنَ الْغَيْرَةِ مَا يُحِبُّ اللَّهُ وَمِنْهَا مَا يَكْرَهُ اللَّهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/ibnmajah:1996@]{.citex}  
    [There is a kind of jealousy what ;Allah; loves, and a kind that ;Allah; dislikes.]{.trans}

(@) [الْفَجْرُ فَجْرَانِ فَجْرٌ يَحْرُمُ فِيهِ الطَّعَامُ وَتَحِلُّ فِيهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَفَجْرٌ تَحْرُمُ فِيهِ الصَّلَاةُ وَيَحِلُّ فِيهِ الطَّعَامُ]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/hakim:687>]{.citex}  
    [There are two dawns: a dawn in which food is forbidden while prayer becomes permissible, and a dawn in which prayer is forbidden while food becomes permissible.]{.trans}

### Using an interjection

Sometimes it hard to come up with any of the above solutions, as in the sentence: "There is a god."
Such sentences, if they are able to be converted to an interjection, may be expressed with the ;mubtada; itself as a one word sentence^[@cantarino_smap@ 1/5 citing @brock_grund@ 2/35]:

(@) [إِلَـٰهٌ]{.ar}  
    "[There is] a god!"

(@) [**أَمِيرَانِ** هَلَكَ الْقَوْمُ]{.ar}
    [cited by @brock_grund@ 2/6]{.citex}  
    [[There are] two commanders?! The people are destroyed!]{.trans}

Consider also this example:

(@ex000141) [سَأَلْتُ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ صلى الله عليه وسلم هَلْ رَأَيْتَ رَبَّكَ قَالَ نُورٌ أَنَّى أَرَاهُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:178@]{.citex}  

The statement
[نُورٌ أَنَّى أَرَاهُ]{.ar}
has been explained by Ibn Taymiyyah with the following wording^[[@ibn_qayyim_juyoosh@ 21](https://shamela.ws/book/18632/80)]:

> [كَانَ ثَمَّ نُورٌ ، أَوْ حَالَ دُونَ رُؤْيَتِهِ نُورٌ فَأَنَّىٰ أَرَاهُ.]{.ar}  
> "There was light, or light came between [my] seeing Him, so how could I see Him?"

This solution should only be considered if the sentence makes sense as an interjection, and can not be used as a blanket solution. For example, the question:

"Is there food?"  

can be expressed in Arabic, not as an interjection, but as:

(@) [هَلْ مِنْ غَدَاءٍ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:2052d@]{.citex}  
    [Is there any food?]{.trans}  

Similarly:

(@) [هَلْ مِنْ مُسْتَغْفِرٍ هَلْ مِنْ تَائِبٍ هَلْ مِنْ سَائِلٍ هَلْ مِنْ دَاعٍ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/muslim:758e@]{.citex}  
    [Is there one seeking forgiveness? Is there a penitent? Is there an asker? Is there a caller?]{.trans}


### Negative and past existential sentences

English also uses the word "there" with this existential meaning for sentences like:

+ "There was a king."
+ "There is no hope."

These sentences can be expressed in Arabic without indefinite ;mubtada;s. For example:

(@) [كَانَ مَلِكٌ.]{.ar}  
    [There was a king.]{.trans}  
    This uses the _self-sufficient_ [كَانَ]{.ar}. (See section\ \@ref(self-sufficient-kaana).)

(@) [لَا أَمَلَ.]{.ar}  
    [There is no hope.]{.trans}  
    This uses the [nAfiyah lil-jins]{.trn2} [لَا]{.ar}. (See section\ \@ref(la-nafiyah-lil-jins).)

### Legitimate use of [هُنَاكَ]{.ar}, ;thamma; and [يُوجَدُ]{.ar}

If, of course, a place is intended by "there" then there is no problem using [هُنَاكَ]{.ar} or its synonyms.
It would appear that they are still locatives, though there may be a suggestion of the existential.
For example:

(@) [كُنَّا نَادِينَ بِالصَّبَاحِ **وَهُنَاكَ** عَبْدُ اللَّهِ بْنُ عَمْرٍو **وَكَانَ هُنَاكَ** امْرَأَةٌ مِنْ بَنِي الْمُغِيرَةِ يُقَالُ لَهَا فَاطِمَةُ فَسَمِعْتُ عَبْدَ اللَّهِ بْنَ عَمْرٍو يَقُولُ «ذَاكَ يَزِيدُ بْنُ مُعَاوِيَةَ»]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/hakim:8461>]{.citex}  
    [We used to meet socially in the morning and [#eabd]{.trn2} ;Allah; ibn [#eamr]{.trn2} would be there. And there was a woman there from [#banU al-#mugIrah]{.trn2} called [#fATimah]{.trn2}. And she heard [#eabd]{.trn2} ;Allah; ibn [#eamr]{.trn2} saying, "That is [#yazId ibn #mueAwiyah]{.trn2}."]{.trans}  

(@) [**أَثَمَّ** لُكَعُ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2122@]{.citex}  
    [Is [the] little chap there?]{.trans}

(@) [جَلَبْتُ أَنَا وَمَخْرَمَةُ الْعَبْدِيُّ بَزًّا مِنْ هَجَرَ فَأَتَيْنَا بِهِ مَكَّةَ فَجَاءَنَا رَسُولُ اللَّهِ ﷺ يَمْشِي فَسَاوَمَنَا بِسَرَاوِيلَ فَبِعْنَاهُ **وَثَمَّ** رَجُلٌ يَزِنُ بِالأَجْرِ فَقَالَ لَهُ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ ﷺ زِنْ وَأَرْجِحْ]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:3336@]{.citex}  
    [I and Makhrafah al-Abdi imported some garments from Hajar, and brought them to Mecca. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ came to us walking, and after he had bargained with us for some trousers, we sold them to him. There was a man who was weighing for payment. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ said to him: Weigh out and give overweight.]{.trans}

(@) [كَانَ رَسُولُ اللهِ ﷺ إِذَا سَجَدَ **وَثَمَّ** بَهْمَةٌ أَرَادَتْ أَنْ تَمُرَّ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ تَجَافَى]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:26809>]{.citex}  
    [When the Messenger of Allah ;pbuh; used to prostrate, and there was a lamb that wanted to pass infront of him, he would have his arms to the side.]{.trans}  

(@) [رَأَيْتُ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ ﷺ يَرْمِي الْجِمَارَ عَلَى نَاقَةٍ صَهْبَاءَ لَيْسَ **ثَمَّ** ضَرْبٌ وَلَا طَرْدٌ وَلَا إِلَيْكَ إِلَيْكَ]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/darimi:1942>]{.citex}  
    [I saw the Prophet ;pbuh; stone the Pillar, on the Day of Sacrifice, from atop a reddish-brown camel of his; and there wasn't any beating or driving them off or [telling them to] go away.]{.trans}

(@) [أَنَّ رَكْبًا وَقَفُوا عَلَى عُثْمَانَ بْنِ عَفَّانَ فَمَدَحُوهُ وَأَثْنَوْا عَلَيْهِ **وَثَمَّ** الْمِقْدَادُ بْنُ الْأَسْوَدِ فَأَخَذَ قَبْضَةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ فَحَثَاهَا فِي وُجُوهِ الرَّكْبِ]{.ar}
    [<https://hadithunlocked.com/ahmad:23826>]{.citex}  
    [... that a group of riders came by [#euvmAn ibn #eaffAn]{.trn2} and praised him. And [al-#miqdAd ibn al-E#aswad]{.trn2} was there. So he took a handful [of dirt] from the ground and threw it in the faces of the riders.]{.trans}


Similarly, [يُوجَدُ]{.ar} may be used with no problem if the meaning "is (to be) found" is intended. For example:

(@) [مَنْ قَتَلَ مُعَاهَدًا لَمْ يَرَحْ رَائِحَةَ الْجَنَّةِ، وَإِنَّ رِيحَهَا تُوجَدُ مِنْ مَسِيرَةِ أَرْبَعِينَ عَامًا]{.ar}
    [@https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3166@]{.citex}  
    [Whoever killed a person having a treaty with the Muslims, shall not smell the smell of Paradise though its smell is perceived from a distance of forty years.]{.trans}  

### Technical and scientific use of [يوجَد]{.ar}

Our above directive to avoid the use of [يوجد]{.ar} to mean "there is" holds for normal sentences.
Sometimes, however, a more technical meaning of "exists" is intended, especially in the language of science.
<!--
However, scholars, in developing branches of science, need to define terminology and use it in the precise manner which their science demands.
Historically, scholars have added on scientific meanings to existing words. There is no problem with this if done carefully and discriminately.
-->
In this case, [يوجد]{.ar} and its [ism mafeUl]{.trn2} [موجود]{.ar} may be used when needing to discuss the existence of something in a scientific text.
But such usage should be restricted to its domain, and should not, ideally, spill over to normal sentences, where a simple "there is" is intended.

By the way, the disused verb [أَيْسَ]{.ar} [to be]{.trans},
(theorized, along with [لَا]{.ar}, to be the origin of the verb [لَيْسَ]{.ar}^[@wilmsen_croft@ 329--331; @wright@ 1/96C])
is also used to express the existence of an object in philosophical works.

<!--
While this concession can be granted to [يوجد]{.ar}, we find no such justifying circumstance for using [هناك]{.ar} existentially.
-->

<!--## [#DamIr al-faSl]{.trn2} after [ism al-istifhAm]{.trn2}-->

## Pronoun of separation after question nouns

In later writing we find the following forms:

[مَنْ هِيَ زَيْنَب؟]{.ar}  
Who is Zaynab?

[مَا هُوَ ٱلْإِسْلَامُ؟]{.ar}  
What is [#islAm]{.trn2}?

[لِمَاذَا هُوَ حَزِينٌ؟]{.ar}  
Why is he sad?

The insertion of the pronoun of separation ([هُوَ]{.ar}, [هِيَ]{.ar}, etc.) after question nouns is best avoided.
So it is better to say:

[مَنْ زَيْنَب؟]{.ar}  
Who is Zaynab?

[مَا ٱلْإِسْلَامُ؟]{.ar}  
What is [#islAm]{.trn2}?

As for "why" sentences, Arabic has a native expression using [مَا لِ]{.ar} followed by a noun or pronoun in the ;icase; and then an adjective in the ;acase;.
For example:

[مَا لَهُ حَزِينًا؟]{.ar}  
Why is he sad?

Another option,
that is sometimes possible,
is to convert the "why" question into a "what" question, thus:

[مَا أَحْزَنَهُ]{.ar}  
Why is he sad?
(literally: What has made him sad?)

## "What happened to ..."

When asking the question, "What happened to a person/thing?" the verbs [فَعَلَ]{.ar} and [صَنَعَ]{.ar} are used and the person or thing asked about is made the ;faa3il;.
For example:

[يَا أَبَا عُمَيْرٍ مَا فَعَلَ النُّغَيْرُ]{.ar}  
What happened to [al-nugayr]{.trn2}  
bukhari:6129

[ما فعل أصحابك]{.ar}  
What happened to your companions?  
riyadussalihin:30

[مَا فَعَلَتِ الرَّيْطَةُ]{.ar}  
What happened to the thin cloak?  
ibnmajah:3603

[فَيَسْأَلُونَهُ مَاذَا فَعَلَ فُلاَنٌ مَاذَا فَعَلَ فُلاَنٌ]{.ar}  
So they ask him: What happened to so-and-so? What happened to so-and-so?  
nasai:1833

## about

The word "about" is often indiscriminately translated as [عَنْ]{.ar}.
[عَنْ]{.ar} is to be used, but only in specific circumstances.
For example, with  the verb [سَأَلَ]{.ar}.

[سَأَلَهُ عَنْ أَمْرٍ.]{.ar}  
He asked him about a matter.

We have also found [عَنْ]{.ar} in the narration:

[ مَا هَذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُونَ عَنِ الْحَسَنِ]{.ar}  
abudawud:4621

Usually, though, the preposition [فِي]{.ar} should be used instead. For example:



## "What about ..."

Avoid 
[مَاذَا عَنْ]{.ar}
when asking "What about someone/something?"

Instead use 
[مَا بَالُ]{.ar}.

For example:

[حَتَّى إِذَا فَرَغَ مَشَيْتُ، فَقُلْتُ مَا بَالُ الْعَظْمِ وَالرَّوْثَةِ ]{.ar}  
bukhari:3860

[إِذَا تَوَاجَهَ الْمُسْلِمَانِ بِسَيْفَيْهِمَا فَكِلاَهُمَا مِنْ أَهْلِ النَّارِ ". قِيلَ فَهَذَا الْقَاتِلُ، فَمَا بَالُ الْمَقْتُولِ]{.ar}  
bukhari:7083

[عَنْ عَائِشَةَ، أَنَّهَا قَالَتْ يَا رَسُولَ اللَّهِ مَا الشَّىْءُ الَّذِي لاَ يَحِلُّ مَنْعُهُ قَالَ الْمَاءُ وَالْمِلْحُ وَالنَّارُ " . قَالَتْ قُلْتُ يَا رَسُولَ اللَّهِ هَذَا الْمَاءُ قَدْ عَرَفْنَاهُ فَمَا بَالُ الْمِلْحِ وَالنَّارِ]{.ar}  
ibnmajah:2474

When the question "What about ...?" is asked as a follow-up to a previous statement then the asked about person/thing can be asked by itself in a single word.
For example:

[عَنِ النَّبِيِّ صلى الله عليه وسلم أَنَّهُ نَهَى أَنْ يَشْرَبَ الرَّجُلُ قَائِمًا . قَتَادَةُ فَقُلْنَا فَالأَكْلُ فَقَالَ ذَاكَ أَشَرُّ أَوْ أَخْبَثُة]{.ar}
"what about eating?"
muslim:2024b

[قَالَ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ صلى الله عليه وسلم لِلْمُتَلاَعِنَيْنِ " حِسَابُكُمَا عَلَى اللَّهِ أَحَدُكُمَا كَاذِبٌ لاَ سَبِيلَ لَكَ عَلَيْهَا " . قَالَ يَا رَسُولَ اللَّهِ مَالِي]{.ar}  
"What about my wealth?"
abudawud:2257

## To make someone do something

In order to express the meaning: to make someone do something, we have a few options:

1. To use a causative verb, like form\ 4, if one is available with this meaning.
   For example:

   (@) [أَدْخَلْتُهُ ٱلدَّارَ]{.ar}
       [@muqtadab@ 2/104]{.citex}  


2. To use the expression [حَمَلَهُ عَلَى أَنْ يَفْعَلَ]{.ar}.
   For example

   (@) [وَقَالُوا شَيَّأْتُهُ ; أَيْ حَمَلْتُهُ عَلَى أَنْ يَشَاءَ]{.ar}
       [@3akbari_tibyan@ 1/37]{.citex}  

   (@) [وَأَعْجَلْتُهُ بِالْأَلِفِ حَمَلْتُهُ عَلَى أَنْ يَعْجَلَ]{.ar}
       [[المصباح المنير للفيومي]{.ar} root [عجل]{.ar}]{.citex}  

   This expressions is especially common in questions like "What made you ...?"
   For example:

   (@) [مَا حَمَلَكَ عَلَى مَا صَنَعْتَ]{.ar}
       [@https://sunnah.com/abudawud:2221@]{.citex}  

3. To use the verb [جَعَلَ]{.ar}.
   For example:

   (@) [جَعَلْتُهُ يَدْخُلَهَا]{.ar}
       [@muqtadab@ 2/104]{.citex}
